title_en: The Correct Understanding of Allah's Names and Attributes
title_ar: The Correct Understanding of Allah's Names and Attributes
title_id: Pemahaman yang Benar tentang Nama-Nama dan Sifat-Sifat Allah
content_id:
Pertanyaan teologis sentral yang dibahas al-Aqeedah al-Hamawiyyah adalah bagaimana seorang Muslim harus memahami dan berhubungan dengan nama-nama dan sifat-sifat ilahi yang disebutkan dalam Al-Qur'an dan Sunnah yang autentik. Ibnu Taimiyyah mulai dengan menegakkan bahwa Allah Yang Mahatinggi telah menggambarkan diri-Nya dalam Kitab-Nya dan melalui lisan Rasul-Nya shallallahu 'alaihi wa sallam dengan nama-nama dan sifat-sifat yang nyata, benar, dan layak bagi keagungan-Nya. Mengingkari ini atau mengosongkan deskripsi-deskripsi ini dari maknanya adalah bentuk ta'thil (pengosongan) yang tidak diizinkan oleh agama.

Ayat kunci dalam diskusi ini adalah firman Allah dalam Surah al-Syura (42:11): 'Tidak ada sesuatu pun yang serupa dengan Dia, dan Dia adalah Maha Mendengar lagi Maha Melihat' (laysa kamithlihi syay', wa Huwa al-Sami' al-Bashir). Ibnu Taimiyyah mencatat bahwa ayat ini secara bersamaan menafikan keserupaan apa pun antara Allah dan ciptaan-Nya sambil menegaskan dua sifat ilahi yang nyata — pendengaran dan penglihatan. Inilah, ia berargumen, model Al-Qur'an dalam berbicara tentang Allah: tegaskan apa yang Dia tegaskan, nafikan apa yang Dia nafikan, dan jangan melampaui teks dalam kedua arah.

Di antara sifat-sifat yang dibahas dalam risalah ini adalah istiva' Allah (menetap atau naik) di atas 'Arsy-Nya, sebagaimana disebutkan di tujuh tempat dalam Al-Qur'an; tangan-Nya, yang disebutkan dalam ayat-ayat seperti QS. al-Fath: 10 dan al-Ma'idah: 64; wajah-Nya, yang dirujuk dalam QS. al-Baqarah: 115 dan al-Rahman: 27; turunnya ke langit terendah setiap malam, sebagaimana ditetapkan dalam hadis-hadis mutawatir; dan kedatangan-Nya pada Hari Pembalasan, yang disebutkan dalam QS. al-Fajr: 22. Ibnu Taimiyyah membahas masing-masing dengan kutipan dari ulama awal yang menegaskannya secara konsisten dengan tanzih Allah.

Dia sangat berhati-hati dalam membedakan antara dua makna kata ta'wil sebagaimana digunakan dalam tradisi Islam. Makna pertama — menjelaskan hakikat nyata atau hasil dari sesuatu — adalah sah dan digunakan dalam pengertian ini dalam Al-Qur'an itu sendiri. Makna kedua — menafsirkan ulang suatu teks dari makna zahirnya karena adanya kontradiksi yang diduga dengan akal — adalah apa yang dipraktikkan oleh teolog-teolog Asy'ariyyah dan Mu'tazilah kemudian dengan teks-teks sifat, dan inilah penggunaan kedua yang dikritik Ibnu Taimiyyah sebagai bid'ah.

Ibnu Taimiyyah mengungkapkan bahwa menegaskan sifat-sifat ini tidak mengharuskan seseorang mengetahui modalitasnya yang tepat (kayfiyyah). Sebagaimana setiap Muslim menegaskan bahwa Allah itu Hayy (Maha Hidup), 'Alim (Maha Mengetahui), dan Qadir (Maha Kuasa) tanpa mengetahui hakikat pasti dari kehidupan, pengetahuan, dan kuasa-Nya, demikian pula sifat-sifat Wajah, Tangan, Turun, dan Istiva' di atas 'Arsy ditegaskan dalam realitasnya tanpa menentukan modalitas bagi mereka. Perbedaan antara sifat-sifat adalah soal tingkat keakraban — beberapa nama seperti al-Hayy terasa lebih abstrak dan karenanya lebih mudah diterima, sementara yang lain seperti al-Yad (Tangan) terasa lebih konkret dan karenanya mengundang salah tafsir. Namun pendekatan yang benar adalah konsisten untuk semua: tegaskan, jangan serupakan, jangan nafikan.

Bab risalah ini meletakkan fondasi doktrin yang mengalir ke seluruh karya: nama-nama dan sifat-sifat Allah adalah nyata, bermakna seperti yang terlihat dalam konteks kesempurnaan ilahi, dan kewajiban seorang Muslim adalah menegaskannya sebagaimana Allah dan Rasul-Nya menegaskannya — dengan iman, takzim, dan pengakuan bahwa Allah sama sekali tidak serupa dengan ciptaan-Nya.
content_en:
The central theological question that Al-Aqeedah al-Hamawiyyah addresses is how a Muslim should understand and relate to the divine names and attributes mentioned in the Quran and authentic Sunnah. Ibn Taymiyyah begins by establishing that Allah, the Most High, has described Himself in His Book and through the tongue of His Messenger, peace be upon him, with names and attributes that are real, truthful, and befitting of His Majesty. To deny this or to empty these descriptions of their meanings is a form of negation (ta'til) that the religion does not permit.

The cornerstone verse in this discussion is the statement of Allah in Surah al-Shura (42:11): "There is nothing like unto Him, and He is the All-Hearing, the All-Seeing" (laysa kamithlihi shay', wa huwa al-Sami' al-Basir). Ibn Taymiyyah notes that this verse simultaneously negates any likeness between Allah and His creation while affirming two real divine attributes — hearing and sight. This, he argues, is the Quranic model for speaking about Allah: affirm what He has affirmed, negate what He has negated, and do not go beyond the text in either direction.

Among the attributes discussed in this treatise are Allah's istiwa' (settling or rising) over His Throne, as mentioned in seven places in the Quran; His Hand, mentioned in verses such as 48:10 and 5:64; His Face, referenced in 2:115 and 55:27; His descent to the lowest heaven each night, as established in mutawatir hadiths; and His coming on the Day of Judgment, mentioned in 89:22. Ibn Taymiyyah addresses each of these with citations from the early scholars who affirmed them in a manner consistent with Allah's transcendence.

He takes great care to distinguish between two meanings of the word ta'wil as used in the Islamic tradition. The first meaning — explaining the real nature or outcome of something — is legitimate and is used in this sense in the Quran itself. The second meaning — reinterpreting a text away from its apparent meaning due to a perceived contradiction with reason — is what the later Ash'arite and Mu'tazilite theologians practiced with the attribute texts, and it is this second usage that Ibn Taymiyyah criticizes as an innovation.

Ibn Taymiyyah makes the point that affirming these attributes does not require one to know their precise modality (kayfiyyah). Just as every Muslim affirms that Allah is Living (Hayy), Knowing ('Alim), and Powerful (Qadir) without knowing the exact nature of His life, knowledge, and power, so too the attributes of the Face, Hand, Descent, and Settling over the Throne are affirmed in their reality without specifying a modality for them. The difference between attributes is one of degree of familiarity — some names like al-Hayy feel more abstract and therefore easier to affirm, while others like al-Yad (the Hand) feel more concrete and therefore invite misinterpretation. But the correct approach is consistent for all: affirm, do not liken, do not negate.

This chapter of the treatise lays the doctrinal foundation that runs through the entire work: the names and attributes of Allah are real, they mean what they appear to mean in the context of divine perfection, and the Muslim's duty is to affirm them as Allah and His Messenger have affirmed them — with faith, reverence, and the acknowledgment that Allah is utterly unlike His creation.
content_ar:
The central theological question that Al-Aqeedah al-Hamawiyyah addresses is how a Muslim should understand and relate to the divine names and attributes mentioned in the Quran and authentic Sunnah. Ibn Taymiyyah begins by establishing that الله, the Most High, has described Himself in His Book and through the tongue of His Messenger, peace be upon him, with names and attributes that are real, truthful, and befitting of His Majesty. To deny this or to empty these descriptions of their meanings is a form of negation (ta'til) that the religion does not permit.

The cornerstone verse in this discussion is the statement of الله in Surah al-Shura (42:11): "There is nothing like unto Him, and He is the All-Hearing, the All-Seeing" (laysa kamithlihi shay', wa huwa al-Sami' al-Basir). Ibn Taymiyyah notes that this verse simultaneously negates any likeness between الله and His creation while affirming two real divine attributes — hearing and sight. This, he argues, is the Quranic model for speaking about الله: affirm what He has affirmed, negate what He has negated, and do not go beyond the text in either direction.

Among the attributes discussed in this treatise are الله's istiwa' (settling or rising) over His Throne, as mentioned in seven places in the Quran; His Hand, mentioned in verses such as 48:10 and 5:64; His Face, referenced in 2:115 and 55:27; His descent to the lowest heaven each night, as established in mutawatir hadiths; and His coming on the Day of Judgment, mentioned in 89:22. Ibn Taymiyyah addresses each of these with citations from the early scholars who affirmed them in a manner consistent with الله's transcendence.

He takes great care to distinguish between two meanings of the word ta'wil as used in the Islamic tradition. The first meaning — explaining the real nature or outcome of something — is legitimate and is used in this sense in the Quran itself. The second meaning — reinterpreting a text away from its apparent meaning due to a perceived contradiction with reason — is what the later Ash'arite and Mu'tazilite theologians practiced with the attribute texts, and it is this second usage that Ibn Taymiyyah criticizes as an innovation.

Ibn Taymiyyah makes the point that affirming these attributes does not require one to know their precise modality (kayfiyyah). Just as every Muslim affirms that الله is Living (Hayy), Knowing ('Alim), and Powerful (Qadir) without knowing the exact nature of His life, knowledge, and power, so too the attributes of the Face, Hand, Descent, and Settling over the Throne are affirmed in their reality without specifying a modality for them. The difference between attributes is one of degree of familiarity — some names like al-Hayy feel more abstract and therefore easier to affirm, while others like al-Yad (the Hand) feel more concrete and therefore invite misinterpretation. But the correct approach is consistent for all: affirm, do not liken, do not negate.
